# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Lesser known movies with good amount of nudity and eroticism

**Score:** 85
**Body:** I am trying to find lesser known movies(more of non-english films) with good amount of nudity and erotic tone throughout.

Few of my favourite movies of this genre in recent years are: 1. Simple Passion(2020)(Dir: Danielle Arbid) 2. The Wait(2021)(Dir: Aku Louhimies) 3. Les salopes(2018)(Dir: Renee Beaulieu) 4. The book of delight(2020)(Dir: Marcela Lordy) 5. Motel Destino(2024)(Dir: Karim Ainouz) 6. Queen of hearts(2019)(Dir: May el-Toukhy)

Not interested in films where nudity is present but is non-sexual and sometimes gory.

Expecting few gems from the comments. TIA!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Pillow Book
2. The Lover
3. I'll list some titles featuring very well known actors but to my knowledge, these movies aren't very widely known today. Due to reasons such as the age of the movie, how much more high budget work was done by the cast members and such. I liked all of these as narratives and not just for the nudity depicted. 

1. Così come sei (1978) - It features late Italian screen legend Marcello Mastroianni paired with then teenaged Nastassja Kinski. I watched an English dubbing, it's originally an Italian language title. But I feel it may be well known to cinema lovers of a certain age or interest cohort. 

2. Lucía y el sexo (2001) - This features a lot of nudity but is a very good narrative too. Some known cast members include Paz Vega, Elena Anaya, Najwa Nimri. 

3. My name is Tanino (2002) - Pretty funny movie. Also has a lot of nudity starring a very young and pretty Rachel McAdams. Watched it only to watch Ms McAdams but it was pretty funny otherwise too. 

4. Habitación en Roma (2010) - I mean both co lead actors are naked in the movie poster. I don't recall much but it was a decent movie. Elena Anaya and Natasha Yarovenko feature nude. 

5. Young Adam (2003) - Features Emily Mortimer nude and Ewan MacGregor, Tilda Swinton among other cast members. 

6. 2 days in the valley (1996) - Features an ensemble cast including Charlize Theron and Teri Hatcher

7. The war zone (1999) - Actress Lara Belmont has a lot of nude scenes although it's not an erotic movie, other cast members include Tilda Swinton, Ray Winstone and Colin Farrell

8. Single white female (1992) - This movie is pretty well known, just kinda old. It features Jennifer Jason Leigh in a lot of nude scenes alongside Bridget Fonda (also in some nude scenes). It's one of Jennifer Jason Leigh's all time greatest roles in a long career with hordes of great roles, including ample nudity.

9. Maria Bello has acted nude in multiple great movies - A history of violence (2005) directed by David Cronenberg is one of her best roles. Again, this is a very well known movie, maybe not exactly what OP is after. 

10. The muse (1998) features ample nudity from Sharon Stone - decent enough for a one time watch, also features Andie MacDowell.
4. - Bitter Moon (1992)
- The girl on a motorcycle (1968)

OP wrote the wait from aku louhimies, I would add Levottomat from him as well.
5. Sliver
6. Choses Secretes.


Beautiful film, it's like the French Eyes Wide Shut.
7. Cashback (2006). i don't know if it's lesser known but it's one of my favorite movies.
8. Room in Rome (2010)  
Lie with Me (2005)
9. 9 Songs (2004)

According to the Guardian, 9 Songs was the most sexually explicit mainstream film to date, largely because it includes several scenes of real sexual acts between Margo Stilley and her co-star, actor Kieran O'Brien. Her role is highly unusual in that she had unsimulated and very graphic sex with O'Brien, including genital fondling, female masturbation, with and without a vibrator, penetrative vaginal sex, cunnilingus, footjob and fellatio.
10. Nymphomaniac Vols. 1 & 2

Body Heat

Henry & June 

Last Tango in Paris

The Dreamers

Belle du Jour

The Piano Teacher

## 2. Guys hit me with some good suggestions for some quality artsy fartsy movies that really blew your socks off

**Score:** 43
**Body:** I'm being serious this is not a troll post. lol Was thinking about watching City of God or The Deer Hunter or Boogie Nights or Paris Texas (never seen any of these films), but I thought maybe I'll dredge up some other suggestions from you fine people! I like all the classic modern guys like Scorsese, Kubrick, Coppola, Tarantino, Coen Bros., Wes Anderson, ect., but I want something more independent. It doesn't need to be abstract, just films that are lesser known and had a lot of passion put into them and are beautifully made and most importantly are good movies. The sorta stuff that cool film students in 2005 would watch  when they were trying to one up each other. lol Non-American films would be a bonus! I've watched and enjoyed stuff like Come and See and Stalker so I'm willing to watch bizarre and or intense films. Doesn't necessarily have to be "artsy" but anything out there and experimental or just cool indie movies that flew under the radar.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. waking life
2. Brazil - Terry Gilliam
3. Moon


4. Ghost Dog (1999)
5. The Thin Blue Line by Errol Morris.  It's a documentary about a murder in Dallas County and a wrongful conviction.  Phillip Glass does the soundtrack.
6. Poor Things
7. The City of Lost Children - 1995

Amélie - 2001
8. Holy mountain
9. Koyaanisqatsi
10. Cinema Paradiso.

## 3. Psychological thrillers with no/minimal gore?

**Score:** 36
**Body:** My fiance and I are trying to find a movie to watch. We both love horror/psychological thrillers, but I’m not good with too much gore. Thanks in advance for any suggestions!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I might be wrong, but I think Rear Window? Come to think of it, probably any Hitchcock.
2. The Others
3. The Game with Michael Douglas 
4. Usual suspects…. Very good
5. Skeleton Key
6. Prisoners 

Wind River 

The Prestige
7. The changling
8. One of the best psychological thillers with no gore is the classic 1944 film

Gaslight starring Ingrid Bergman and Charles Boyer.
9. Spoorloos (1988) English name: The vanishing
10. Copycat

## 4. Movies for my soon-to-be-graduating teens

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Dearest Reddit,

I’m looking for movies to watch with me (the mom), my husband (the stepdad) and my 18 year-old twin sons. We watched Ferris Bueller when school started, and they dug it. Looking for something similar to show them this weekend. The hardest part is finding teen flicks without boobs. I mean, we all love them, but who wants to watch a movie like that with your mom.

They enjoy and understand the significance of 80’s greatness like Goonies.

Edit: Lots of great ideas. Thanks my dudes. (Yes they’ve seen Bill and Ted’s. I am a good mom. )

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Can't Hardly Wait


Say Anything


The Lost Boys
2. Dazed And Confused, if you don’t mind beer and weed and errr the threat of paddling? I’m pretty sure there’s no boobs.
3. Real Genius (1985)  
Game night (2018)  
Be kind, rewind (2008)  
10 things I hate about (1999)  
Easy A (2010)
  - +1 on Real genius. To tie in the degrees of separation may I suggest wargames? Same theme and Matthew Broderick continues his genius
4. Tropic Thunder is raunchy fun. 

Galaxy Quest for genuine lols with big heart. 

Napoleon Dynamite may have dated references for them, but it’s another classic.
5. I’d go with Point Break with Patrick Swayze and Keanu Reeves. Not really a teen movie as much as a classic 90’s actioner but it is not to be taken seriously and the only boobs are non sexual ones during a shower fight.
6. The Sure Thing
7. Dead Poets Society

The Goonies (never mind I see you have that covered!)

Stand By Me
8. Without a Paddle (2004). We watched it as a family and did laugh a fair bit. It does contain drug (marijuana) references but it's all in good fun.
9. Best in Show
Heathers (even embraced by my grouchy, anime teens)
Dead Poets
Adams Family/Adams Family Values

## 5. Lonely,Homesick and experiencing nicotine withdrawals . Suggest me a movie

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I’m alone in my room right now. My roommates went home and I really wish I could go home too, but I live far away and it’s not possible at the moment. On top of that I think I’m experiencing nicotine withdrawal, so I’m kind of restless and low at the same time.
The movie doesn't have to be feel good or comforting just anything you think i might like in such state.


### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "thank you for smoking" 
  - Came here to say this
2. Ive been having nicotine withdrawals for 7 months,when does it fucking stop. I was much nicer and happier when I was vaping,but I’ll never go back.
  - Physical nicotine addiction withdrawals only take a few days to clear out of your system, but the mental addiction aspect is much harder to break. Try associating something else, any hobby that keeps your hands busy that you can quickly pick up when the cravings hit helped me(2 pack a day for 12 years before quitting) but even after 6 years I still get a major craving here or there. Everyone is different but I know I could never touch nicotine in a casual way again
3. Home Alone

Homeward Bound 

Cast Away with Tom Hanks 

The Martian
4. Watch Lord of the Rings trilogy Extended edition
5. "Cold Turkey" Never saw it but it's about quitting smoking with Dick Van Dyke. 

Hang in there. It only takes two full days to quit. 
6. mamma mia gets me going especially if you sing along! 

and if you're feeling lonely, watch brooklyn nine nine, I watch it when I'm lonely and it really comforts me
7. The Warrior 2001 film
8. 1945	The Lost Weekend

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Eric Dane passes away at 53. May he rest in peace. 💔

**Score:** 906
**Body:** Around one year since he announced his ALS diagnosis. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That was a horrifically quick descent from initial diagnosis. Glad it was quick for him, but that must feel like absolute whiplash for his kids and loved ones
2. First Catherine, then James, now Eric.   Man these past few weeks are just heartwrenching.  Rip ❤️ 🙏🏽
  - Robert Duvall as well. This year’s off to a horrible start 💔
    - Rob Reiner
3. The other McSteamy. Sad news, so young
  - He was the only McSteamy. 

Dempsey was McDreamy.
4. I will let my wife find out for herself in the morning, I don't want her crying all night. Rest in peace Eric.
5. Truly sad.
6. I heard a rumor that he like to play villains, the reason being they didn’t have to kiss costars and he wanted to respect his wife.
  - Unless I’m misremembering (it’s been years since I watched) a lot of Euphoria, he must’ve changed his stance on this, if it’s actually true.  He was great on that show (I didn’t see ER).

No judgement—it’s just acting.

I wish his family well, this has to be incredibly difficult for them. 

Fuck ALS.

## 2. What’s your favorite Michael Keaton film and performance?

**Score:** 339
**Body:** Performance: Dopesick

Film: Night Shift

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|PnTbJDFX0q9IDKnqyx)
2. Film: Batman

Performance: The Founder
3. Captain Gene

The Other Guys

I can’t believe this movie is already 15 years old
  - Don't go chasing waterfalls
4. Ray Nicolette in Jackie Brown

6. 220 221 whatever it takes.
7. Spotlight … come on now. Founder close second

## 3. Wuthering Heights: I've never seen a movie miss the source material so hard.

**Score:** 295
**Body:** Finally saw Wuthering Heights last night. Read it as a youth, and rewatched a Miniseries adaptation in anticipation for the upcoming film.

First, I'll say I'm no purist and if a director has a specific angle or inroad to a story no ones more open to hear than me.

But I will say I do know there is a staunch fandom for Bronte's work and is many's favorite.

And yet, watching Emerald Fennel's vision for this film unfold didn't scandalize me for it's heightening of existing sexual aspects of the book of the book- it alarmed me that this director genuinely did not understand what made this story tick.

Look I'm not mega "woke" but Wuthering Heights is nothing if not being all about the details and in this story Heathcliff, whatever his nationality may be is distinctly "Other".

He is a man of some distinct origin and it plays a heavy role in the overarching narrative.

More importantly this is a story of two people in love who are torn apart by contemporary circumstance, two innocent children in love who have to face society and the times and navigate that and the story that ensues tears their souls apart.

Style wise- Looking at the execution Fennell is clearly going for some kind of meta adaptation, like a 60's Soap adaptation of the story youd see on British television. If that were the case I would have encouraged her to just go al the way on that concept and make it fully look like that down to the cinematography. Because while i actually love stylized adaptations her portrayel really just reads as a high school drama play that didnt have the budget nor could be bothered to differentiate from time periods.

None of it comes across as cool or stylish it feels silly and pointless.

What's so sexy about Wuthering heights is its about raw desire coming up against the rigid class rules of society, but here Emerald reduces it all down to drooling, mindless hornyness.

This is what summed all it up for me: We find Heathcliffe at one point with Isabella, we find that he has subsituted his love for Cathy with a vile perverse relationship with Isabella instead.

Ok, so this part is way heightened here with Heathcliffe walking Isabella around on a leash and treating her like a literal dog. Here's the thing- I'd be okay with this choice if we were able to see that Heathcliffe looked absolutely ruined at this point. Just a hollow shell trying to chase the fix he could never get with Cathy and now a depraved husk taking it out on this woman.

But no, Heathcliffe seems more than okay than to be doing this which is where I have the problem. He's NOT torn apart by anything at least in the reading of the scene.

And the last, "best" part of the whole affair is when Cathy finally succumbs to illness the movie ends.

Sorry, no. Heathcliffe living on, being haunted by her ghost IS a crucial part of this story and excluding it almost begs to just go ahead and change the name of the movie, because Wuthering Heights IS a ghost story.

Overall, Fennell absolotely acts like a reader who skipped through this novel missing every character driver and nuance and just tapped into the most surface level, cliffs notes level scenes to make her version which is totally devoid of any weight or meaning.

Seriously if she had just been fauthful to the story she'd have this hot, erotic, heavy ass story that would have had people drooling.

I almost wonder if she didnt mean to adapt a different story even, like Jane Eyre or something. She could have taken many other stories and given it this treatment probably to a high level of success- or hell- even just created a totally unique period drama that aped on all of these period dramas.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is an interesting debate that keeps coming up about adaptions. Whether a movie needs to be faithful to the book and how much breaking from it is acceptable. 

I think if the quality of the movie in its own right is great enough it can get away with ignoring huge chunks or the intention of the source material. 

An example being the Shining. The Kubrik movie is widely said to be bad as an adaption, yet in its own right is a critically acclaimed film.

But if the film is bad or average even then you have a negative/lukewarm reception on top of fans of the source material thinking great potential has been squandered. 

Having seen this version of WH, and loving the book, personally I think it's a weak use of the incredible source material for such a big director with this much resources available. But life goes on. There will be other adaptions.
  - Felt this way about American Psycho. Doesn’t capture the book, but a great movie so idec.
2. One thing that's sticking out to me is that Fennell keeps emphasizing in interviews that what she's made is pretty much what she imagined when she read this book at age 14. So it's a highly stylized, disjointed, horny adolescent mess that ignores a lot of the themes and the entire second half of the story *because* that's what her brain was doing back then.

And I think that makes a lot of sense. I found the romance novel I tried to write at 14 and it's hilarious! There's a dungeon! And a forced marriage! And a truly weird amount of focus on what everyone was wearing? I would set myself on fire before letting it see the light of day.

Anyway, as much as I hated this movie, it made me appreciate Saltburn more in retrospect for being a highly stylized, disjointed, horny mess.
  - A romance novel with a dungeon and arranged marriages would probably do numbers these days. Just call it A Cave of Vines and Flowers or some shit. 
  - Taika Waititi basically did this for the movie Jojo Rabbit. His mom was reading a book and tried to explain the plot to him and the movie is what he *thought* the book was about. It's not faithful to the book at all but it's still an amazing movie.
  - Maybe she should have made a movie other people would want to see, not just one she wanted to
3. None of the adaptations of *Wuthering Heights* are true to the source material. This one doesn’t even try. Apparently that’s why there are quotation marks around “Wuthering Heights” in the promotional material like the official poster.

The source material isn’t crowd pleasing Hollywood material. The only movie I am think of that tells the story of selfish, mean, obsessed, destructive, and self-destructive lovers is *Sid and Nancy* (1986). 

While that low-budget independently produced movie eventually developed a cult following, it was not a box office success. It’s not the kind of movie others seek to emulate. 

But I recommend it, and I’m curious if any other fans of Emily Brontë’s *Wuthering Heights* see a resemblance. I’m also curious if anyone can think of any other movies that feature such unlikable lovers.

Wait, I just thought of another movie that told the story of toxic lovers — *Double Indemnity* (1944). Of course, that was a story of murder that no one misinterpreted as a romance. But it was also the story of a toxic romance.
4. I knew I wouldn't like it from reading the cast list and finding out that, like a lot of adaptations, they skipped the *whole second half of the book*.
5. There was an interesting podcast about the same thing a week or so ago. I was never going to see this film but after reading the podcast transcript, I felt like I had dodged a bullet. It sounds utterly awful, emphasizing the toxic relationship to the exclusion of everything else, and that relationship is the reason I despise the story. I think now of all the young women who will crave the negative excitement of an awful relationship and it just horrifies me. At least if you read the book you'll get a glance at the generational harm done. Ugh.
  - I have only read the book and that was many years ago, but I have always been a bit mystified that the overall story is now being thought of as a romance because of my interpretation of the book was the romantic couple were at the very least deeply problematic and probably just outright toxic by most standard’s and the core of the story was revenge, so that’s kind of how I think of it, as a revenge story. 

As I said I have never see any adaptation but I do understand that some of them have chosen to focus more on the first part of the book, in which case, I could see how that could be pushed in the direction of a romance, though I still think the relationship, and probably the two character’s involved themselves are themselves deeply, deeply flawed and probably toxic people at their core. 

To me it was a compelling drama because these two were both so messed up, it felt destined to be a miserably horrible train wreck of a story but the exact kind you can’t pull away from…but not really one I would consider a “romance” at its core if that makes sense.

## 4. Favorite debut movie by a debutant director after 2000. My pick is James Wan’s Saw (2004).

**Score:** 154
**Body:** Saw is one of my favorite movies from James Wan, and it had an incredible twist at the end.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Saw is so misrepresented as some torture porn film when the first film is actually a really good crime thriller which reminds me of something like Seven, not as good but still excellent, particularly for a debut and he went on to direct insidious and conjuring, I have a lot of time for James Wan personally

Edit - I’ll go for Robert Eggers the Witch
2. Alex Garland - Ex Machina (2014)

![gif](giphy|19OIUml06udBC)
  - I’m gonna tear up the fuckin dance floor dude check it out
3. In Bruges by Martin McDonagh(He had made a short film before this though, called Six Shooter)
  - https://i.redd.it/r3xi6oqdgokg1.gif
4. Absolutely : Lock Stock &two smoking barrels by Guy Ritchie.
5. Maybe its.... JJ Abrams. Mission Impossible 3 (2006) is pretty Awesome. And the Cold open before the opening credits goes incredibly hard..
6. Jordan Peele - Get Out
7. In Bruges by Martin Mcdonagh

Promising Young Women by Emerald Fennell
8. The original Saw was such a mind fuck. Loved it. 
Unfortunately subsequent ones were less successful and focussed more on gore.

## 5. Which director’s cuts were major improvements over their theatrical versions?

**Score:** 152

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't think there's a better example than Kingdom of Heaven. The theatrical cut is just an ok movie, the director's cut is sensational and takes the film to a whole higher level. Even more so bc Eva Green has much more screen time.

I think the director's cut of Troy is generally a bit superior to the theatrical version, but the soundtrack during the fight between Achilles and Hector is terrible, I have no idea why this was changed. Peter O'Toole has a much more prominent role and the film features more dialogue and extended battle scenes.
2. Blade Runner
3. Dark City - the director's cut gives the movie a lot more space to breathe and doesn't spoil the whole fucking thing with a stupid voiceover in the first 2 minutes. 
4. Not saying people need to think it’s good, but the ultimate cut of BvS was undeniably an improvement with better pacing, a more coherent plot, and more screen time for title characters like Supes, who feels like a supporting character in his own movie in the theatrical cut

![gif](giphy|3o7WTF9ficFIvvRyeI)
5. Star Trek: The Motion Picture
6. Another Ridley Scott movie: I greatly prefer the director's cut of "Legend" (1985).
7. Haven't seen The Abyss mentioned but there is a huge difference between cuts.
8. Not technically a directors cut, but the Assembly Cut of Alien 3 is really good. 

Although I kinda like the ending of the theatrical cut better.
9. Zach Snyder’s Justice League Director’s Cut
10. "major improvements over their theatrical versions" gives the impression of the theatricals being bad, which is absolutely untrue, but the extended/directors cuts of Peter Jackson's LoTR trilogy are really the best way to go. But I also understand the studio not wanting to release a trilogy where each movie is over 3 or 4 hours long, especially in the early 2000s.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. There should be a “recommended point in your life” rating for movies. This can help prevent you from watching films prematurely, and maximize your enjoyment and appreciation of the directors vision and message.

**Score:** 260
**Body:** My father was quite a cinephile growing up. This lead to him exposing me to quite a few classics from a young age. And while I greatly appreciate his desire to educate me, I also regret that I saw some movies way too early. Many movies, I now in hindsight wish I could see again with fresher more mature eyes. 

Sadly, watching these movies older, all while knowing how the plot unfolds really deprived you from capitalizing on your own experiences to truly immerse yourself in what you’re watching. 

On top of that, it might tarnish your original impressions of said film just because you might lack the emotional maturity, or life experiences to relate to the characters. 

Obviously I’m not telling people to wait until they lose a family member to watch Manchester by the sea, but I genuinely wish I hadn’t seen many movies until now. The big lebowski for example I watched when I was 17 and I think that was a huge mistake. 

I believe some of the criterion for my new rating system that could be used are:

Watch when you’ve started stagnating in life and seek purpose. 

Watch when you’ve had children

Watch when you’ve had to care for an adult 

Watch after you’ve experienced love, heartbreak and healing. 

I’d love to hear what other metrics you’d consider in your personal rating system. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A couple of contrary thoughts: I have never once thought it was a mistake to have seen something early and then years later with a new understanding. Those are the moments that give you the best insight into how much you have changed, they are very important. Plus to me, films are meant to be rewatched and lived with; the first watch isn’t all that significant and grows increasingly less so with time.
  - One of the most pleasant and surprising experiences is watching movies as a child and seeing it through the eyes of one of the young characters. Then rewatching as an adult and seeing it through the eyes of the older characters. Especially films with a child-parent dynamic. 

And also my favorite Lost In Translation (2003). For years I saw myself as Scarlett Johansson's character, with Bill Murray's character emotional turmoil as a little bit alien to me. Then one day, 20 years later, re-watching and I'm him, looking at ScarJo like the way I look back at my younger self.
    - Those little scenes with Bill’s wife calling about carpet colors and home decor questions are relatable now. I overlooked them until recently. Those little details strengthen his character’s fall into the abyss of a boring job, international travel demands, seeking adventure, and Scarlett’s pull. 

Similarly, I watched Fincher’s The Game recently. I hadn’t seen it in a long time. The protagonist leads a wealthy workaholic lifestyle until his world is turned upside down. I see the movie in its literal sense and as a perspective of someone experiencing loss of control and no time to grieve. He is on a roller coaster of extreme events, and they are relentless. Completely empathize with his character now as an adult just trying to hold on.
  - I just typed a *way too long* comment to share the sentiment you expressed much more succinctly here!
2. I don’t believe that films are ruined once you’ve seen them because you know how the plot unfolds. For me, only the first viewing is about the plot. Everything else about appreciating the art is for rewatches, which means there’s nothing wrong with revisiting films later in life.
  - agreed. And indeed, watching a movie you haven’t seen since you were young *is still* often like watching an entirely new movie.
3. My father tried to get me to sit through Lawrence of Arabia on video when I was a kid and I thought it was the most boring thing I had ever seen. 

Skip ahead 40 years and I asked my father if he'd like to see the newly remastered version on the big screen.  I 100% expected it would be a labor of love on my part and I'd be able to get a nap in.

Turns out he was right and I was captivated, because by that time I'd seen enough movies to recognize it was a masterpiece.
  - A fucking masterpiece is what it is. It's got the best cinematography. And in this house Lawrence of Arabia is a hero. End of story.
4. I do agree with you, I watched films when I was too young that went over my head. The great thing is though many films speak to you as a teenager then you find new meanings in when your older on a rewatch. 


5. This appeal for some "pure" viewing experience, or dismay that your original viewing will be "tarnished" feel like very silly things to waste your energy on.

No two experiences of watching a film will ever be the same, does one ultimate experience tarnish all the others?

Re-watching a film through a new set of eyes has always been exciting for me.  It gives me an appreciation for where I once was and where I am now.

There's nothing wrong with reflecting on your growth.

## 2. Just finished watching Terrance Malick's movies

**Score:** 70
**Body:** What an incredible journey it has been working my way through Terrance Malick's films. From the historical epics like The Thin Red Line and A Hidden Life to the loosely constructed 'weightless trilogy', I am a big fan of both sides of this director. Being a huge fan of Tarkovsky's Mirror, I was pleasantly surprised to see how this was referenced in Tree of Life, which is so far the only Malick film I have seen twice!
I was surprised how much I loved To The Wonder as I heard this film didn't click with some fans at the time. Knight of Cups felt like the most dense of Malick's films, and it took me some time to process everything that was going on. I have to admit that while I liked the free flowing poetry of the weightless trilogy, this approach did start to lose me in Song to Song, perhaps I had watched these films too close together, or maybe this one felt a little too abstract. Either way, I am fully open to watching this again.
So then I moved onto A Hidden Life, a return to a narrative-focused Malick film was refreshing, but I really enjoyed the experimental camera work that Malick had become known for in recent years.

Without a doubt Malick is now among my top 10 directors. As a recent convert to Malick's work I am curious to know which films do you like the most, which ones benefit from rewatches? Have you been surprised by how much a film has resonated with you after a second or third viewing?

My current ranking (although I am sure this will change upon repeated viewings).

1. The Tree of Life
2. The Thin Red Line
3. The New World
4. A Hidden Life
5. To the Wonder
6. Days of Heaven
7. Knight of Cups
8. Badlands
9. Song to Song
10. The Voyage of Time

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The new world is one of my absolute favorite movie, and I rewatch it all the time. 

Thin red line and the tree of life are both incredible.

Even the “bad” Malick movies have breathtaking moments in them.
  - I've only seen the 3 hour cut of The New World. I heard it was a divisive film, but I absolutely loved it! So many breath taking moments in that film
2. I’m a huge Malick fan, so thanks for this write up! Yes, Tree of Life is incredible and deserves the top spot without question. 

I’m actually surprised that you put To The Wonder above Days of Heaven, as the latter get so much love from the fans, and the former was Malick’s most tedious film for me. It’s been ages since I saw it last. I’ve been planing a full rewatch myself soon, and I’m eager to see if my feelings change on that. 

I’m so pleased you put A Hidden Life so high, as I consider that one an underrated gem. It’s such a beautiful movie with an incredible message and had me ugly crying by film’s end.
3. I saw Days of Heaven when I was very young. I remember Linda Manz. "Me my brother. It used to be just me and my brother." I saw and understood that film through the eyes of that naive young girl. The complexities of the relationships were way beyond my life experience. I just wanted them all to be happy. I remember how magical everything was but, at the same time, how strange and lonely I felt in the midst of all that beauty. To this day Saint Saëns' Aquarium sends me right back to that feeling of mystery and dread and not understanding how all this could be so beautiful and so terrible. I wore out my vinyl sound track. It was the background score of my early teens.
I have not revisited this film since. The memory is my innocence preserved in amber.
4. Pretty good ranking. I'd personally put Song to Song at the bottom since I've tried to watch it twice and never made it through without falling asleep. I'd bump Voyage of Time up probably above Knight of Cups but then it feels bad to put Badlands so low.
5. Where you recommend someone start with Malick? I've only seen Tree of Life and it was...an experience, but not a bad one. though some of it was probably lost on me. My attention span for movies is a bit shot but I was able to watch The Deer Hunter and was captivated the whole time. 
  - I'd recommend starting with Days of Heaven. It's an hour and a half long and feels more fully realised than Badlands, which is also a great movie, but perhaps less indicative of the style that Malick is known for.
  - If I were you, I'd start with *Badlands* and *Days of Heaven*. They're his earliest, obviously, and they're both tight, concentrated, well under two hours, relatively straightforward. But already "Malick." 

When he produced nothing else for 20 years after *Days of Heaven*, he built up quite a mystique, like a cinematic Salinger. That speaks to the spell those two films cast. It seemed that every male actor working in the '90s wanted to be in *The Thin Red Line*. Some big names actually did shoot scenes that were left on the cutting-room floor, like Mickey Rourke.
  - See Badlands. It's a far more conventional movie while still feeling like Malick. And it's excellent. 
6. *Days of Heaven* has always been my favorite, but I think all of his first five have a reasonable claim on masterpiece status.

I hope you have seen the extended *Tree of Life* he put together for the Criterion release, too. It's a very different experience: more prose to go with the poetry, and some good opportunities for Pitt and Chastain. I actually prefer this version, and not everyone is with me on that, but a big fan of the film should see both.

*Song to Song* plays like there might be a great miniseries in there. He shot a lot of footage for it. I get the sense that Holly Hunter is giving a performance as great as any she's ever given, but we're only getting fragments of it.

## 3. Mr. Nobody Against Putin and sometimes the truth

**Score:** 16
**Body:** I got excited about *Mr. Nobody Against Putin* after learning it was nominated for an Oscar for Best Documentary Feature. However, while watching it, I noticed that the creators take certain episodes out of context to fit a specific narrative.

At one point, a student named Masha talks about how her brother is doing after he was mobilized to fight in the war in Ukraine. In the subtitles, the filmmakers use the word "*drafted*." Then, Pavel says in a voice-over (quoting the subtitles): "As rough as it was to see Masha's brother go off to war, it wasn't any easier to see my own students get *drafted*."

In the following sequence, we see friends and family gathered to send Pavel's former student, Ivan, off to the army. The sequence ends with tragic music and a shot of a rainy day. Pavel mentions that he couldn't fall asleep for a long time. We then see a shot of him kneeling by his bed with his head buried in the sheets.

Later in the movie, we see a going-away party for another guy named Nikita before he is sent off to the army. In a voice-over, Pavel says: “'I will see you when you get back.' 'See you in a year.' But some guys will never come home.” Pavel then proceeds to talk about his classmate, Artyom, who was killed in Ukraine. We listen to a recording from his funeral, which I found very disturbing and sad.

Both of these scenes are directed, edited, and narrated in a way that makes the viewer think Ivan and Nikita were sent to the war in Ukraine. But this is not true. I believe they were called up for mandatory conscription, which is a very different thing from being deployed to Ukraine.

A few things tell:

1. Ivan says he wants to serve in the Kremlin, which some conscripts actually do.
2. One of the guys mentions that Ivan is leaving for a year—which is the standard duration for conscription. Mobilization, however, doesn't have an expiration date.
3. The subtitles for the voice-over read: "my own students get *drafted*." But a literal translation of the Russian would be "go to the army," a phrase commonly used for routine conscription.
4. It looks like the sequence with Ivan was shot in late spring or summer, and the one with Nikita was shot during the winter. However, the actual Russian mobilization took place from late September to November.

Getting conscripted is a much more common occurrence. It is mandatory for all men aged 18 to 30, and hundreds of thousands of men go through it every year. Conscripts don't take part in the invasion of Ukraine or any other armed conflict. Honestly, it's a pretty routine thing in Russia.

This is why I find it misleading to present the sequence with Ivan right after Masha talks about her brother in Ukraine, especially using the exact same word "*drafted*" in the voice-over. Ending this sequence with tragic music and sad shots of the rain and Pavel crying in his sheets—honestly, it felt so overdramatic that I actually found it hilarious. And why would you say, "But some guys will never come home" right after showing a party for Nikita, who will very likely be back in exactly 12 months?

There are also other smaller manipulations, but post already got too big. Take [this](https://imgur.com/a/wCvQSPb), for example.

I understand why the filmmakers did this. It completes their narrative about how the government feeds students propaganda about the war in Ukraine: the students listen to the propaganda, finish school, and immediately go to war. Without these sequences, that narrative falls apart, and we wouldn't know whether all these mandatory classes and the teachers' bullshit are actually successful.

It's not a bad movie, there's real heart there, but there are serious ethical implications with the way the footage was used endangering the people involved without informed consent to make some kind of point about Russia invading Ukraine causing blowback within Russia.

## 5. The Double Life of Véronique (1991)

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I just saw this movie and I do not understand the appeal. I felt nothing while watching this movie. Completely disconnected and disengaged.

  
To me this movie just felt like a bunch of random clips stitched together. Perhaps the first "part" of the movie with Polish Veronika made a bit of sense but that all went through the window when we moved to French Véronique.

  
I'm not trying to be a troll, I'm genuinely asking for your opinion in good faith, what do you like about this movie? Why did it resonate with you? Am I missing something? I might rewatch it later.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. To me it was everything in that film: the beautiful cinematography, the performances, the music, the idea of the entanglement between the two unknown women and the symbolism of how the traumatic experiences of the one saved the other, the fact that it doesn't try to explain everything and instead leaves open questions and lets you think and imagine for yourself (that's a quality that I love in films). It is one of my favorite movies ever, and the only one that when it ended, for a moment I seriously thought to replay it from the begginning, right away. 
2. This is one of my favorite films. To me, it explores destiny and how it shapes the characters’ lives. The tension lies in how much power individual choice and moral decisions hold within that reality. 

Weronika is devoted to her art at the expense of both her personal life (Antek) and her health (heart condition). She feels compelled to sing in such a powerful way that she becomes cut off from her emotions and physical limits. She disregards the universe’s warning signs and her life ends tragically.

Véronique is more connected to her inner world. She is in tune with her emotions and the universe. When she senses grief and anxiety (echo of Weronika’s death), she withdraws from singing. When she receives the tape, she actively listens. Ultimately, one of them lives and the other does not. Would Weronika live if she had chosen a different path and allowed herself to feel, the way Véronique decides to do, or was she always headed towards a tragic end? Are their choices truly free, or are they guided by fate? These questions are left open ended.

The two puppets, one of them a “spare”, symbolize the two women and the consequences of their choices. Weronika sacrifices herself for art, allowing Véronique to live. If you choose to read it that way, Weronika can be interpreted as the “first pancake” of the universe, the one that fails so the next can succeed. 

There is also historical context. The film is set in the 1980s, when Polish artists were subject to censorship and political pressure, which often constrained their artistic expression. Western European artists were allowed to operate with greater freedom. So the two main characters can be read as reflections of these two realities. Kieślowski always insisted that political commentary was not his main goal with this film, but I think it’s an interesting point of view to consider.
3. I also disliked it. It’s been a long time since I saw it, but it seems I experienced the film in a similar way. Of course, a good way of finding films you’ll like is to ‘go back to the well’ of the filmographies of directors who’ve made something you did like (such as The Three Colors trilogy). But sometimes that doesn’t work out, and it can be extra frustrating, because there would seem to be some puzzle or incongruity about why The Double Life of Veronique for instance doesn’t work.

Maybe Kieslowski doesn’t understand or empathize with the Veronicas in the way that he might with the protagonists of the Three Colors films? Veronique is more of a decorative, phantasmagoric trifle. (I remember watching a behind the scenes featurette, and it’s the crew re-setting between takes of a scene in which Irene Jacob is on a divan topless, or something like that, and Kieslowski seemed a bit aloof, ironic, dictatorial from far off beside the camera. They are exchanging some words, I think. I don’t remember, but I thought it looked like Jacob was trying to be cool and laughing, while also looking embarrassed.)

4. Maybe I don't understand nothing about cinema but I find Krzysztof Kieślowski overrated, both Veronique and color trilogy are boring and felt nothing watched them, there are indeed original concept in them but I felt the purpose was simply to add something new rather than explain something
